query: most important role of cholesterol in plasma

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic defects that lead to increased LDL levels in the blood include genes that regulate LDL receptors in the liver. LDL receptors mediate the uptake of LDL into the liver. Endocytosis of LDL is the primary way that the body decreases cholesterol levels, so it follows that a decrease in LDL receptor function would also increase LDL concentrations in the blood. Patients with a genetic disposition for high cholesterol can be placed on cholesterol-lowering medications like statins to decrease their risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia is a disorder in which there are high cholesterol levels in the blood. Three main conditions result in hypercholesterolemia: Polygenic hypercholesterolemia: The most common disorder to raise cholesterol levels; there are many genes involved that elevate LDL concentration in plasma Familial hypercholesterolemia: An autosomal dominant disorder in which the gene for the LDL receptor is defective, so removal of LDL from plasma is less than effective Familial combined hyperlipidemia: Discussed previously above

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Diet has a variable effect on cholesterol levels among individuals. [9] However, it has been shown that diets high in saturated fats and trans fats can increase cholesterol in the blood. [10] Dietary cholesterol has not been shown to contribute significantly to LDL concentrations, but its role is still in question. [11] [12] [13] Lifestyle changes such as regular aerobic exercise can also help control cholesterol levels. Saturated fats should not comprise any more than 7% to 10% of the diet.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Ultimately, LDL can be recycled in the liver cells’ Golgi apparatus to make more lipoproteins, or it can get excreted through bile. The “empty” HDL can enter the circulation to pick up excess cholesterol from the tissues. This transfer is mediated by ABCA1, and mutations to ABCA1 genes carry links with low serum HDL cholesterol and familial HDL deficiency, Tangiers Disease. HDL can also acquire cholesterol from cells via SR-B1 or passive diffusion and directly transport cholesterol to the liver by interacting with hepatic SR-B1 or indirectly by transferring cholesterol to VLDL or LDL, facilitated by CETP. Lecithin cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) which is activated by apoA1 on HDL and mediates the esterification of cholesterol in HDL. Other ABC transporters may carry implications in cholesterol metabolism. Sitosterolemia is a rare genetic disorder that presents with elevated plasma levels of plant sterols, including sitosterol and premature atherosclerosis. Mutations to the ABC half transporters, ABCG5 and ABCG8, can increase intestinal uptake of plant sterols since there is a failure to efflux plant sterols into the lumen. Overexpression of the ABCG5 and 8 lead to increased biliary cholesterol secretion and lead researchers to suspect that they may regulate biliary cholesterol secretion. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Since cholesterol is mostly a lipophilic molecule, it does not dissolve well in the blood. For this reason, it is packaged in lipoproteins that have phospholipid and apolipoprotein. [3] Lipoproteins are made up of a lipid core (which can contain cholesterol esters and triglycerides) and a hydrophilic outer membrane comprising phospholipid, apolipoprotein, and free cholesterol. This allows the lipid molecules to move around the body through the blood and be transported to cells that need them. There are several types of lipoproteins that travel through the blood, and they each have different purposes. There are high-density lipoproteins (HDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL). Notably, LDL particles are thought to act as a major transporter of cholesterol; at least two-thirds of circulating cholesterol resides in LDL to the peripheral tissues. Conversely, HDL molecules are thought to do the opposite. They take excess cholesterol and return it to the liver for excretion. Clinically, these two lipoproteins are significant since high LDL and low HDL increase patients' risk of atherosclerotic vascular diseases. [4] [5] See Figure. The Molecular Structure of Cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since cholesterol is mostly lipophilic, it is transported through the blood, along with triglycerides, inside lipoprotein particles (HDL, IDL, LDL, VLDL, and chylomicrons). These lipoproteins can be detected in the clinical setting to estimate the amount of cholesterol in the blood. Chylomicrons are not present in non-fasting plasma.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hypolipoproteinemia refers to relatively low levels of lipids in the blood. Such a condition may be associated with a genetic component or perhaps other conditions like anemia or an overactive thyroid. Hypolipoproteinemias include three primary conditions: Hypoalphalipoproteinemia: A reduction in HDL cholesterol levels in plasma that is associated with a greater risk of coronary heart disease Abetalipoproteinemia: An autosomal recessive disease; it is caused by apoprotein B deficiency and is characterized by the lack of chylomicrons, LDL, and VLDL in the blood Tangier disease: An autosomal recessive disorder classified by an absence of plasma HDL, which results in the synthesis of abnormal chylomicron remnant

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Statins have an indirect effect on the metabolism of lipoproteins. The mechanism of action involves competitive inhibition of HMG CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway in the liver. As the liver senses a decrease in cholesterol production, it attempts to compensate by increasing the number of LDL receptors on the surface of its cells, which leads to increased uptake of two lipoproteins, LDL and VLDL, into the liver, which then metabolizes into cholesterol and other molecules. Statins effectively decrease the number of circulating LDL molecules and VLDL in the serum, leading to the desired decrease in cholesterol deposition into tissues. [18] Other medications include ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors in addition to statins to lower LDL-C greater than 50%, especially in high-risk patients. Newer drugs that target cholesterol and lipoprotein metabolism will be critical in managing or preventing ASCVD in the future.

retrieved_docs.title: Kidd Blood Group System -- Clinical Significance -- Effect on Total Cholesterol Levels
retrieved_docs.content: Some studies suggest that individuals homozygous for Jka have higher total cholesterol levels compared to others, and the Jkb gene may have a restrictive effect on the variability of total cholesterol levels. Although there was no effect on high-density lipoprotein cholesterol or triglycerides, the reason has been postulated to be the variability gene. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, LDL Cholesterol -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The LDL receptor is found in the liver and most other tissues. It recognizes Apo B 100 and Apo E, mediating the uptake of LDL, chylomicron remnants, and IDL through endocytosis. After internalization, the lipoprotein particle is degraded in lysosomes, and cholesterol is released. When cholesterol enters the cell, HMG CoA reductase activity increases then synthesizes cholesterol and modulates the expression of LDL receptors. LDL receptors on the liver determine plasma LDL levels. When there is a low number of receptors, less LDL can be taken up from the blood by the liver, leading to high plasma LDL levels. Conversely, when there are more LDL receptors, more LDL is taken up from the blood by the liver, leading to low plasma LDL levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (high LDL-cholesterol) is one of the major risk factors contributing to the formation of atherosclerotic plaques. These plaques lead to an increased possibility of various negative clinical outcomes, including, but not limited to, coronary artery disease. PAD, aortic aneurysms,  and stroke. A major contributor to the increased risk of atherosclerotic lesion formation is high levels of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) in the blood. Also, it has been shown that an elevated high-density lipoprotein (HDL) blood concentration is correlated with a decreased risk in epidemiological studies, but clinical trials with therapies that elevate HDL-cholesterol have yielded null results. For this reason, a major focus of patient care is primarily to decrease LDL levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Low Density Lipoprotein -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The LDL particles are cleared from serum through the interaction with the LDL receptor. The outer membrane of the LDL contains a phospholipid monolayer, unesterified cholesterol, and Apo-B protein, whereas the inner core contains a highly nonpolar CE. The cholesterol is transported via blood and endocytosed in the target tissues via LDL receptor-mediated endocytosis. When LDL binds with its receptor in plasma membranes, small invaginations called caveolae, consisting of caveolin and the receptor proteins, are formed and endocytosed via clathrin-coated pits and vesicles. Once endocytosed, the LDL receptor recycles to the coated pits while the cholesterol is transported to lysosomes, where the hydrolases degrade the apo-B protein to amino acids and cleave the CE to cholesterol and fatty acids. The cholesterol is either incorporated into the cell membrane or reesterified and stored as lipid droplets via the action of ACAT. When mutations are present in the LDL receptor, normal uptake of LDL by the liver is impeded, leading to familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) with markedly increased LDL cholesterol levels, whose inheritance is autosomal dominant.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Low Density Lipoprotein -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Both cholesterol esters (CE) and triacylglycerols (TG) are insoluble in water (plasma); hence they are packaged as lipoproteins comprising an inner core of CE and TG with a surface coat of apolipoproteins, free cholesterol (FC), and phospholipids. Packaged as lipoproteins, they can be transported to various tissues where needed. By differing the lipid and protein concentrations, up to 5 different lipoproteins can be produced, such as chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). LDL is the predominant carrier of serum cholesterol (around 67%) and delivers it to tissues of need such as the adrenal glands, gonads, and other tissues. With a density of 1.019 to 1.063 g/ml, LDL contains 20% protein and 50% cholesterol (CE and FC) and displays beta mobility on electrophoresis. This review focuses on LDL, especially its biochemistry, measurement, and clinical significance. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The interaction between water-fearing and fat-loving displays more clearly during lipid transport in plasma. Both cholesterol and triglycerides are nonpolar lipid molecules. Therefore, they must travel in the polar plasma with the help of lipoprotein particles. The main goal of lipoprotein is to help transport lipids (hydrophobic) in water. The structure of lipoprotein consists of triglycerides, cholesterol, phospholipids, and apolipoproteins. Apolipoproteins mainly function as carrier proteins but also serve as cofactors for enzymes that metabolize lipoproteins and help in lipid component exchange among lipoproteins. Some examples of lipoproteins include chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). Each one is used in a different phase of lipid transport. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The Neiman-Pick C1-like-1 protein (NPC1L1) is a human sterol transport protein expressed at the enterocyte/gut lumen and hepatobiliary interface. This protein has a sterol sensing domain that facilitates the internalization of free cholesterol into the enterocyte. When extracellular cholesterol is high, cholesterol is incorporated into the cell membrane and is sensed by the NPC1L1, which internalizes the cholesterol through interaction with the AP2-clathrin complex. The lipid-lowering medication ezetimibe prevents this interaction and thus leads to reduced plasma LDL-cholesterol levels. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cholesterol -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Cholesterol fulfills several biological functions and is necessary for successful cellular homeostasis. It acts as a precursor to bile acids, assists in steroid and vitamin D synthesis, and plays a central role in maintaining cellular membrane rigidity and fluidity. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 848
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin are all members of the therapeutic class identified as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing overall cardiovascular risk. Because they share the same mechanism of action, these statins are commonly used in the management of hyperlipidaemia and for primary or secondary prevention of atherosclerotic disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and potential drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2780
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are a specific category of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified as hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). These conditions involve abnormalities in the synthesis, transport, or breakdown of lipids, leading to elevated blood lipid levels and associated risks such as atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease. Recognizing disorders of lipid metabolism as both a metabolic disturbance and a form of hyperlipoproteinemia underscores their role in systemic metabolic health and guides targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and pravastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels in patients. As members of the same therapeutic category, fluvastatin and pravastatin share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hyperlipidemia and reduce cardiovascular risk. Recognizing that both agents belong to the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor, plain lipid‑modifying drug class helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual patient factors while understanding their shared pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K is classified as a fat‑soluble vitamin, meaning it belongs to the broader group of vitamins that dissolve in lipids and require dietary fat for optimal absorption. This places vitamin K within the same category as vitamins A, D, and E, highlighting shared characteristics such as storage in fatty tissues and the need for adequate fat intake to ensure proper bioavailability. Recognizing vitamin K as a fat‑soluble vitamin is essential for understanding its absorption dynamics, its role in physiological processes like blood coagulation, and for guiding nutritional recommendations that support its effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 497
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise several specific agents that are members of this drug class. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and pravastatin are all included within the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group, reflecting their shared mechanism of inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase to lower cholesterol levels. Recognizing these agents as members of the same therapeutic class aids clinicians in understanding their common pharmacologic effects and in selecting appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1941
retrieved_docs.content: Above reference range is used as an interpretation when evaluating disorders of lipid metabolism, indicating that laboratory values exceeding the normal limits may reflect underlying lipid metabolic abnormalities. Within the category of disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a specific subtype, representing conditions characterized by excessive circulating lipoproteins. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset prediabetes) is considered a member of the broader disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical interrelationship between dysglycemia and lipid metabolic disturbances. Understanding these connections assists clinicians in interpreting abnormal lipid panels, identifying hyperlipoproteinemic states, and recognizing the metabolic overlap with early glucose dysregulation for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that function by binding bile acids in the intestine, thereby reducing cholesterol reabsorption and prompting the liver to convert more cholesterol into bile acids. Two commonly used members of this class are colestipol and colesevelam, both of which share the same mechanism of action and are employed to help manage hypercholesterolemia, particularly when statin therapy alone is insufficient or contraindicated. By decreasing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, these agents contribute to cardiovascular risk reduction and are an important therapeutic option in comprehensive dyslipidemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic diseases that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. In addition to lipid abnormalities, this category is linked to elevated blood glucose conditions such as new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)), highlighting the close interplay between lipid and glucose metabolic pathways. Recognizing that hyperlipoproteinemia and early diabetes can coexist within the spectrum of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores the importance of integrated assessment and management of both lipid and glucose abnormalities in patient care.
